segments.text: Um, uh, to the Social
Supers and committee, select Committee.
So we have, uh, you for 60 minutes this morning.
Um, uh, please, uh, feel free to introduce yourselves
and, uh, make some introductory, um, comments.
And then, uh, after
that we'll move to questions from members.
I'll try and keep it short. Um, uh, uh, good morning, Mr.
Chair, members of the committee, thank you
for the opportunity to present on social
investment agencies work over the past year.
My name is Alistair Mason.
I'm acting Secretary for Social Investment
and Chief Executive of the Social Investment Agency.
Joining me today is Afra Green,
deputy Chief Executive Assistant
Performance and Investment Advice.
Joe Fowler, deputy Chief Executive
Investment and Commissioning.
And behind me have some other members of staff,
including our CFA, despite significant
and growing investment in social services outcomes
for our most vulnerable remain poor.
Put simply, people with complex needs are not well served
by fragmented and siloed
and siloed system in which services are di divided up
between different agencies, time and again.
They slip through the cracks.
They need integrated solutions delivered early
to prevent a cycle of crisis
intervention throughout their lives.
The role of the SIA is to identify opportunities
to break the cycle to ensure people
with complex needs received the kind of support they need
before the crisis point.
To do that, we're using data to see who's at risk of.
We're trusting communities
and providers to design solutions,
and we're giving them the resources and freedom to deliver.
We're collecting and monitoring evidence
of what's working and what isn't.
In our first year, we have focused on establishing the
foundations for complex multifaceted system change
with a particular emphasis on data, evidence
and targeted investment.
A core part of this work has been opening up
access to data and evidence.
We've developed a regional data explorer tool
that would enable local providers
and commissioners to understand population trends
and outcomes in their communities.
Who needs support, what works, and where the gaps exist.
This shifts us towards evidence
informed practice at a local level.
Social investment requires
clarity about who we're supporting.
So we u we are using data to identify cohorts
at greatest risk of poor outcomes.
We want to understand not just their immediate needs,
but the interconnected challenges they're facing.
This approach allows us to target interventions
where they'll have the greatest impact
and track outcomes over time.
As a central agency
and a system lead, our role to role is
to lead the implementation
of the social investment approach, build the infrastructure
and tools required, and demonstrate how aspects
of social investment can work from practice.
Our engagement with other agencies across the social system
is already bearing fruit, and we are beginning
to see agencies applying the social investment lens
to the commissioning programs.
We'll continue to work to shift the system setting standards
and using data and evidence so the system invests earlier
and more effectively to change lives.
Budget 25 a allocated $190 million
to the social investment fund over four years.
Ministers subsequently decided priority cohorts
in August 25.
Round one. Procurement process was openly advertised.
That process and foundations we allowed laid down over the
24 25 financial year and now paying off.
Most recently, the Minister
for Social Investment announced seven organizations working
with children and families of complex needs that have,
that have been selected to receive
funding through Pathway One.
New initiatives together, they stand
to receive $50 million over four years, delivering programs
to supporting 1600 children and their families per year.
Pathway two will consolidate contracts across multiple
government departments, reducing the sort of fragmentation
that sees providers managing dozens
of separate funding streams,
spending more time writing reports for Wellington,
and less time delivering vital services.
We expect to announce the results
of Pathway two process early in the new year.
Everywhere else work is well underway, scoping
and designing pathways three
and four, which involve local commissioning
and co-investment with iwi, philanthropists,
and community trusts.
This year we are shifting from design to delivery,
implementing outcome-based contracting,
open those partnership pathways, working directly
with agencies to demonstrate
what social investment looks like in practice.
It's worth emphasizing that while our own efforts amount
to only a tiny sum
of the overall SP social services spending,
our work is about leading and demonstrating an approach
to social spending that comes
to redefine the way all social
services are designed and delivered.
With that in mind, we'll continue
to bolster our system lead role with guidance
and standards to embed social investment
principles across the sector.
Ultimately want, we want to put decisions
and the information needed to make them as close as possible
to the people they affect, and
that will be guiding our process in the year to come.
Thank you Mr. Chair. We welcome committee these questions.
Uh, thank you very much. Uh,
first question is from Lawrence.
Uh, thank you chair. Um, thank you for coming
and thank you for the work that you do.
Uh, my first question is just picking up on something you
said where you said Minister decided on priority cohort,
how then, because the idea
of the agency is a long-term focus
and you said outcome focused.
Mm-hmm. What happens then when the minister change their
mind and then they decide to prioritize something else?
How then would you measure the outcome?
Our role is to present the best
advice we can to ministers.
So they decide the particular cohorts.
Um, we implement, uh, the decisions that they make,
but our advice is very transparent about
how we came across those cohorts,
how we selected those cohorts, um, and gave that advice.
They are some of the most vulnerable populations in this
country who are likely
to have the most long term poor outcomes.
Anything you would like to add that Joe?
I think only that, um, the, um,
cohorts we're investing went into four year arrangements,
um, with those organizations to support those changes.
Um, the ambition here is that the fund does grow over time
and that additional investment in further cohorts would be
dependent upon additional funding.
Ultimately, there's a hard edge to the fund. Um,
The Your, your investing in in social services,
you're looking for the return on those.
Um, and continued investment obviously depends upon you
having continued confidence in that return.
Just a couple of supplementary questions
and other people may have subs as well.
Um, but in terms of the outcomes, I'm looking also at your,
um, non-financial performance indicators in the budget.
Nothing in that financial indicated works about outcomes.
It talks about advice that you provide to the thing.
So for example, I've seen that, like for example, on page 19
of your report, you mentioned a lot
of initiatives that you have taken.
Tangible ones. How are you gonna measure the outcomes
and what happens when the money that you've allocated
for it ran out and you didn't
achieve the outcomes you wanted?
Ultimately, you need to use investment logic to test,
to see if we're delivering the outcomes
that we are expecting to see.
We have quite a robust process of the outcomes
and the interim indicators that we would expect to see,
to see if we're making progress.
If it's not working, we need to work with those providers
to see if there is a better way we can do
to deliver those outcomes.
We need regular measurement
and data to see if we are making the most effective, um,
or getting the most effective results.
Recognizing that these providers are working in some
of the most complex situations in the country, um,
these results won't come up in a linear progressive way.
They need to have the time
and the scope to work with those very vulnerable, um,
clients and communities.
Uh, so they, they've got the ability to do that,
but ultimately we do need to be keeping regular tracking
to see if we are making the progress
that we would hope to be doing.
When we go into a, uh, original contract with a provider,
we ask them for it to develop a theory of change that allows
that provider to demonstrate to us,
and we work alongside them of the, I expect results
that they would expect to see
with their particular supports that they can provide.
We can then go and measure that to see if that theory
of change is actually playing out in reality.
Um, that will deliver over time indications
that we are making success.
And if we're not seeing that success, then it's a, it's a
transparent conversation with that provider about
what we can do to, to change tech if we're not delivering
That. One final
supplemental question,
if we can just go into one example on page 19.
You mentioned early year support, autism New Zealand.
Um, this is hugely important. I agree.
Uh, what then would your outcomes be for that?
When do you expect to see some form of outcome?

of autistic children and children selling signs of autism.
The barrier that we have now in terms
of autism is diagnosis.
Diagnosis is hugely expensive.
So is that gonna be also affected? That's addressed in here.

He's been leading this part bit
Of work. Yeah, and it's a great example
to, to walk through.

very early screening tools, um,
and supporting frontline professionals who work, um, with,
with new parents and, and,

that reflect, um, the, the difference

It looks at, um, families

of autistic children
and the strategies they can use to have a positive impact,
um, in terms of long-term outcomes, um, to decre
to decrease child expression of distress
and to reduce parental stress
and increase parental sense of competence and confidence.
Um, ultimately though, um,
and this is again a great example, the actual outcomes

um, who are in, um, the early stages of school
and are more confident and capable
and supported in being there than they would've been
otherwise if they hadn't had those supports, um, through
that, the early phase.
So we are looking, that's also an initiative
that is quite different and it's testing something new
and it's one of the, um, early tests, if you like, of, um,
initiatives that might make a wider change to public service
and social services across the country in time.
Um, if what they're doing works and has a significant impact
and we can clearly see the return on that, then
that's something that you wanna see adopted across the
Country. Your
question, Jen, thank you and thank you for that.
Um, I guess reading the annual report, one thing

And we don't have those people there in the interim

of those smaller providers play a crucial role, um,

look at their contracts so they've got more robustness

of social investment is about prevention.
So, um,
those five cars up the driveway type thing happen usually at
a crisis and we should be there before that.
Um, hopefully stopping those drive cars.
Turning up to the driveway.
Last question, Jen. Well, it kind
of follows on from that.
Um, because this approach has been used successfully
in the past and, and from my perspective, one
of the most successful was called Taro, which some
of you know really well.
Um, and it was again, consolidation
of the different agencies having those discussions,
who was the most appropriate going in.
So it was a real place-based solution.
Can you give assurances that the seven initiatives
that have been announced are place-based to stop that sort
of thing happening as opposed to being program-based
as they've been, um, reported in the media being program.
Uh, that gave me a little bit of, oh my goodness,
are we going back in time
Here? Yep. Could
you judge, please?
Yeah. Um, absolutely.
Um, that initial test of whether that investment is credible
and worth, um, making, we look very strongly
at local relationships.
Um, and we actually published a short list
of the initiatives that we consider funding online
and in asked and asked for feedback.
And we will continue to do that
because of that commitment to transparency.
So by way of example, um, we wanted to know, um,
that if one organization was gonna set up in Hamilton
delivering a service that was particularly
around young people that were getting involved
with the police, um,
that the police were absolutely on board
and we actually facilitated conversations
and tested that to make sure
that those connections were in place.
Um, and that service, um, opened last week.
Um, we are also, um, particularly look to organizations
that are deeply embedded
and have that connection between the operational
and strategic end, which I you remember well.
Um, that ability to understand sit at a table every day
looking at family harm incidents
or, um, children that had reports of concern, understanding
that at that very granular level
and then being able to shape social services
around that intel.
So naty hour social health services in Whakatane
and we've invested in it very much in that space
and are bringing those two operational and um,
and commissioning of services together,
Which is great to hear.
I just think we need to get the language right
Around that because it
Is quite wrong at the moment how it's being reported.
Yeah. Well, well thank, thank you very much Sha
for caring for the clinic today.
You're obviously are keen interest in what you're doing.
Um, we wish you all the success going forward.
Thank you Pat. An important piece of work. Thank you.
Thank you very much.
Cool.
